# **ads**

### view

## ADS

## Collaborative Research: Extreme-scale Ready High-order Methods for Astrophysical and Laboratory Turbulence

#### Abstract

Astrophysical phenomena inherently involve multi-physics and multi-scales that are nonlinearly coupled. The mathematical accuracy and efficiency of the computational tools used to test theoretical models hugely impact the scientific outcomes. The astrophysics community has thus long focused on developing computationally accurate numerical algorithms that give improved solution accuracy, stability, and efficiency. This project tackles the challenge of developing new approaches to address these problems, and incorporating these methods in the FLASH community code in order to reach a large audience in the astrophysics community. The work lies at the nexus of applied and computational mathematics, statistical Gaussian Process (GP) data predictions, and astrophysics. Involvement in NSF-supported outreach will help to educate under-represented community college students in computational astrophysics. Training young scientists in astrophysical and laboratory turbulence using validated simulations also helps to meet a critical national need.This study will develop high-order accurate, novel algorithms with only modest complexity, using a new high-order GP spatial reconstruction approach and a novel single-step high-order temporal integration method. This powerful predictive tool will run on massively parallel high-performance architectures. The study involves, firstly, using the novel GP approach to overcome the second-order bottleneck in most finite-volume shock-capturing astrophysics codes. The GP algorithms provide a simple mathematical framework for computing highly accurate volume-averaged versus pointwise quantities, and multidimensional spatial reconstructions. The second important development is an efficient high-order temporal integration method in adaptive mesh refinement grid configurations. This requires a single-step time update at a single quadrature point per cell face, which will provide the most efficient algorithmic framework in extreme-scale parallel simulations. Finally, comparing simulation results based on solution accuracy, errors, and computational performance for key astrophysical flow problems, including astrophysical and laboratory turbulence, will demonstrate the impact and benefit of these advances.This award reflects NSF's statutory mission and has been deemed worthy of support through evaluation using the Foundation's intellectual merit and broader impacts review criteria.Project Outcomes: This project has developed new algorithmic designs for solving highly compressible astrophysical fluid problems using computer simulations. The project has used a highly accurate numerical method called the kernel-based Gaussian Process (GP) method to solve nonlinear evolution equations of fluid flows. The GP method has been designed to support multiple spatial dimensions, such as 1D, 2D, and 3D physical space dimensions, when solving the governing fluid dynamics equations in computer codes. This approach maximizes the use of multidimensional data information in space in GP's fluid flow modeling. The study showed excellent enhancements in simulating astrophysical flow problems over the existing polynomial-based high-order numerical methods. The project has implemented the kernel-based finite volume GP-WENO method in a fully parallel GPU/CPU code, which has been thoroughly tested against a suite of stringent benchmark problems well-known in the computational astrophysics community. The results showed that the new GP approach could handle highly compressible shocks stably and produce variable high convergence rates. The project has produced four peer-reviewed publications so far, with further papers to appear. Throughout the project period, six PhD students and two MS students were trained in the design of numerical algorithms, and three undergraduate students had research opportunities by participating in summer programs or enrolling in research credits. The project results were presented at more than two dozen conferences, seminars, colloquiums, and other professional meetings. Last Modified: 12/16/2023 Submitted by: DongwookLee

enriched_points.expanded_content: Innovative algorithms, such as kernel-based Gaussian Process methods, are being created to enhance the simulation of complex astrophysical fluid dynamics across multiple dimensions.
New computational techniques, including high-order methods, significantly enhance accuracy and stability in simulating astrophysical flows, addressing challenges in highly compressible regimes.
Machine learning models are being integrated to predict fluid behaviors, optimizing simulations and providing insights into astrophysical phenomena beyond traditional numerical methods.
